questions.question: Indeed __________ the truth
questions.explanation: We need the past tense form of the verb here, eliminating option A. "Held the truth" does not mean anything, making option C incorrect. One cannot "dominate" the truth. It's incorrect contextually, eliminating option D as well. Hence, option B will be the correct answer.

questions.question: Punish for Dharmabuddhi's ________
questions.explanation: We need a word with a negative connotation as the context is that of 'punish'. This eliminates option A and B. Again, we need a noun for the blank, whereas C is a verb. Option D fits the blank grammatically and contextually.

questions.question: Is telling __________
questions.explanation: We need a noun for the blank which will eliminate option B, it being an adverb. D can be outright negated as it does not hold any contextual meaning here. "Trust" and "truth" are two different things and cannot be equated. We're talking about words which can be only true or false. Hence, option A will be the correct answer.

questions.question: Tree and _______ fire to it
questions.explanation: "Put off" means an off-putting or unpleasant quality or feature and will not be true here. We need a word in the past tense, which will eliminate option B. Saying "to throw fire" is incorrect and does not make any sense. Hence, option D will be the correct answer.

questions.question: His body, _________ for pity
questions.explanation: We need a verb in the present participle tense, eliminating option A and option C (adjective). Option D is non-contextual here and can be straightaway eliminated. Hence, option B will be the correct answer.

questions.question: In the below questions, sentence/passage is split into four parts and named P, Q, R & S. These four parts are not given in their proper order. Read the sentence/passage and find out which of the four combinations is correct. P. The man was evidently a foreigner of some sort, he wore a peculiar hat on his red hair, which was neatly brushed. Q. He had a foreign-looking cape round his shoulders, and brightly polished, pointed shoes. R. For some reason he wore bicycle-clips round the bottoms of his trousers, and this made him even more foreign-looking. S. He wore dark glasses on his nose and his cheeks were very bulgy.
questions.explanation: It is clear that sentence P must begin the passage, since it is the only sentence that begins with 'The man..' All of the others refer to him using the pronouns 'he' or 'his', indicating that they form the mid part of the passage. Options A and D are eliminated. Sentences P and Q are a pair, since both sentences talk about the man's clothes. Option B is eliminated. Option C is the right answer.

questions.question: In the below questions, sentence/passage is split into four parts and named P, Q, R & S. These four parts are not given in their proper order. Read the sentence/passage and find out which of the four combinations is correct. P. He simply wandered here and there, making towards the sea, but arriving at a rocky beach where no boats could possibly lie. Q. He was completely hopeless. R. He didn't know the way, and he couldn't find the boats. S. His father was perfectly right.
questions.explanation: Sentences R and Q are a pair: R tells us about the fact that the protagonist did not know the way, and Q describes his feelings on the realization of this fact. Options B and D are eliminated. It is clear that sentence P should follow the pair RQ, since it tells us about his further response, and follows the chronological order of events. Option C is eliminated. Option A is the right answer.

questions.question: A sentence is given with a blank to be filled with an appropriate word. Four alternatives are given for each sentence. Choose the correct alternative out of the four that fits the statement contextually and grammatically. The__________ of our allies is held by rather tenuous ties.
questions.explanation: Allegiance means ties or collaboration or inclination towards a group. None of the other options except (d) fit the context.

questions.question: Four words are given in which only one is correctly spelt. Find the correctly spelt word.
questions.explanation: Tumultuous means confused, disordered etc. Correct spelling is Tu-mul-tu-ous Example: The rumour that there is a bomb in the stadium turned the audience tumultuous. The correct spelling of other words is- Turbulence, Disruption, Interference.

questions.question: A part of the sentence is bracketed. Below are given alternatives to the bracketed part which may improve the sentence. Choose the correct alternative. In case no improvement is required, choose "No Improvement" option. The white tigress in the zoo (has two offsprings).
questions.explanation: The plural form of 'offspring' is offspring itself. There is no words as 'offsprings'. The same word is taken to be plural or singular according to the context. So 'offsprings' is to be replaced by 'offspring'.

questions.question: The sentence below has a portion bracketed. From the options, choose the option that improves the bracketed part in the given sentence. Not long after the preceding incident, (for getting the ache of too constant labor out) of my bones, and to relieve my spirit of the irksomeness of a settled routine, I took a holiday.
options: to get the ache of too constant labour out
for getting out the ache of too constant labor
in order to get the ache of too constant labor out
questions.explanation: Options B and D are eliminated as the present continuous tense, i.e. 'for getting' has not been used in the second clause of the sentence, i.e. 'to relieve'. So, following the rules of parallelism, the same tense should be used for both clauses. Between options A & C, option A is better because it's more direct and conveys the same sense as done by C. Having said that, usage of "in order" is redundant.

questions.question: Four words are given in which only one is correctly spelt. Find the correctly spelt word.
questions.explanation: Ennoble means to give a noble rank or title. The word ennoble has three syllables: en-no-ble Example: In addition to your skill, your commitment ennobles your profession. The correct spelling for other words is- Accredit, Licensed, Privilege.

questions.question: Fill in the blanks I was going to moralize this fable, when our attention was called off to a warm dispute between my wife and Mr Burchell, upon my daughter's intended _________ to the town.
questions.explanation: Option B is eliminated, as it has the word 'cruise', which means a journey taken by ship, while the sentence is not talking about such a journey. Option C is eliminated as we need a noun for the blank, while 'gloomy' is an adjective. Option D is eliminated as it is non-contextual. Option A is the right answer, as it has the word 'jaunt' meaning a short trip made for pleasure; this fits most accurately in the sentence. A is the right answer.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one which can be substituted for the given words/sentences. A story in which people, things or happenings have a hidden or symbolic meaning.
questions.explanation: Fiction: literature that describes imaginary events and people. Sequel: A published work that continues the story of an earlier work. Myth: A story concerning the early history of people, natural or social phenomena, false beliefs or ideas. Allegory: a story, poem, or picture with a hidden message or meaning.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one which can be substituted for the given words/sentences. The quality of having strong moral principles, honesty and decency.
questions.explanation: Pristineness: Clean and fresh as if new. Prefect: A senior pupil who is authorized to enforce discipline. Immaculate: Clean or spotless.

questions.question: Four alternatives are given for the idiom/phrase in bold. Choose the correct alternative that best expresses the meaning of the idiom/phrase. a white elephant
questions.explanation: ' a white elephant' is a possession that is useless or troublesome, especially one that is expensive to maintain or difficult to dispose of. c is the right answer.

questions.question: In each of the following questions, a word in bold letters is followed by four words. Select the word that is opposite in meaning to the word in bold. Dipsomaniac
questions.explanation: Dipsomaniac - is a drunkard or an alcoholic. Opposite of this would be 'sober'.

questions.question: In the following question, a sentence has been given in Active/Passive Voice. Out of the four alternatives suggested, select the one which best expresses the same sentence in Passive/Active Voice. He spent all his free time playing cricket with his friends.
options: All of his free time was spent playing cricket with his friends.
He is spending all his free time playing cricket with his friends.
All of his free time was being spent playing cricket with his friends.
All of his free time is spent playing cricket with his friends.
questions.explanation: The sentence is in active voice and in simple past tense. C is eliminated as it turns the tense into past progressive. B is eliminated as it is also in active voice. D is eliminated as it turns the sentence into simple present tense. A is the right answer, as it turns the sentence into passive voice without altering the tense.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one which is opposite in meaning to the given word. VERACIOUS
questions.explanation: Veracious means speaking truth. Example: Gandhiji always possessed the quality of being veracious and preached everyone to be veracious.

questions.question: Some parts of the sentences have errors and some are correct. Find out which part of a sentence has an error and choose the appropriate option. If a sentence is free from error, choose "No Error" option. All these things happened because she does not hear my words.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence is "All these things happened because she did not hear my words". Since 'happened' is in past tense the rest of the sentence should also be in past tense. But 'does' is in the present tense. So it should be replaced by 'did' which is past tense of 'does'.

questions.question: In each of the questions below, a sentence is given in either direct or indirect speech. Select the option that correctly converts the sentence in direct speech to indirect speech or vice versa. "Although we tried, we couldn't pass the test," said Polly.
options: Polly said that although they had tried, they couldn't pass the test.
Polly said that although they tried, they couldn't pass the test.
Polly said that although we tried, we couldn't pass the test.
Polly said that although they try, they couldn't pass the test.
questions.explanation: The sentence is in simple past tense, and should be converted into past perfect. c is incorrect as 'we' must change to 'they'. b is incorrect as it is also in simple past tense. d is incorrect as it is in simple present tense. a is the right answer.

questions.question: Some parts of the sentences have errors and some are correct. Find out which part of a sentence has an error choose the appropriate option. If a sentence is free from error, choose "No Error" option. Two hundred metres beyond (1)/ that playground was seen (2)/ numerous buildings including some schools. (3)/ No error (4)
questions.explanation: The correct sentence is "Two hundred metres beyond that playground were seen numerous buildings including some schools". Here the subject is "numerous buildings" which is plural and not "playground". So 'was' should be replaced by 'were'.

questions.question: Choose the word similar in meaning to the given word. Plagiarism
questions.explanation: Plagiarism means 'the practice of taking someone else's work or ideas and passing them off as one's own'. The word similar in meaning is 'Piracy'

questions.question: Choose the word similar in meaning to the given word. Obfuscate
questions.explanation: Obfuscate means to make obscure, unclear, or unintelligible. Hence, the word similar in meaning is 'Confuse'

questions.question: Four alternatives are given for the idiom/phrase in bold. Choose the correct alternative that best expresses the meaning of the idiom/phrase. A bee in his bonnet
options: to talk a lot about something
questions.explanation: To have a bee in your bonnet means to be mad at something or keep talking a lot about something because you feel it is important.
